comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few minutes away, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. As I mentioned last week, once I found a publisher willing to bring out my fiction, a lot of it found its way into print in a hurry, so we're going to be in tentacle territory for a while now.  This was my fifty-second published book, and it's another tentacle novel, but it's not a part of the core Weird of Hali sequence. The Shoggoth Concerto came slithering up out of my subconscious while I was hoping to get the rest of the Weird published but things weren't looking especially promising; I thought that an independent novel set in the same fictional cosmos might be able to get a hearing at one of the big publishers...and then I started writing, and it turned into the oddest of my many odd books.

You know all those stories about someone who has brash new innovative ideas and gets bullied by the defenders of tradition?  This is a story about someone who wants to do something traditional and gets bullied by the avant-garde, which is of course much more common these days. It's also about love, memory, magic, and shoggoths, and to my taste, it's still the best of my novels. I did submit it to a big publisher; I actually got a personal response, which is rare, saying that it was too quiet and too weird -- those were the editor's exact words -- but that if I wanted to write something more publishable I could send it directly to her. I rolled my eyes and found someplace else to publish, as bending the knee to the shallow fashionable clichés of big-brand fantasy and SF is the last thing I wanted (or want) to do. If shoggoths or classical music appeal to you, you can buy a copy of The Shoggoth Concerto here if you're in the US and here if you're elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no more comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: Indigo Children Prediction
comments.content:
I’ve been meaning to ask about the “Indigo Children” prediction that floated around sometime before and after the turn of the century. I have no idea how it got started or by whom.

It seems to me that the barebones prediction, stripped of all the grand flourishes about how fabulous, talented, and enlightened these incoming souls were supposed to be, was essential quite accurate. As I recall it was basically that a cluster of souls was 1) supposed to be born (or had been born?) for a short period of incarnation 2) who would have a dramatic impact on society and/or change the world in some lasting beneficial way.

The Indigo Children prediction has been on my mind as very high mortality rates in that specific demographic have featured in the unfolding c0vid debacle. Now, with the impact it (c0vid) seems to have had on events in the current US election cycle, strikes me that this is is very much what was predicted; minus the fantasy about how it was all supposed to come about. Given the dropping birthrates, even the bit about it these souls being only temporarily incarnated seems pretty accurate.

I don’t really have a question, I just wondered what you might think about these speculations.

replies.title: Re: Indigo Children Prediction
replies.content:
I have very mixed feelings about that whole business. On the one hand, it does happen from time to time that groups of souls come into incarnation together for various reasons. On the other...

In every single case I ever encountered of a parent claiming their child was an Indigo Child, the child was being raised by an egomaniac who was projecting her (it was almost always her) fantasies about being specially special with extra special sauce onto the poor kid. It's bad enough when parents do this to kids in some ordinary way -- Joe's going to grow up into the basketball player I could never be, Emmy's going to have the musical talents I wish I had, et cetera -- those are painfully common and result in a lot of misery. But it's potentially much worse when the parent burdens the child with fantasies of supernatural power and a gargantuan mission. I bet a lot of those poor kids desperately wanted to leave home and become fry cooks or something else utterly normal and unremarkable.

The whole thing was invented by New Age writer Nancy Ann Tappe, btw, back in the 1970s, and got picked up and marketed by various other New Age entrepreneurs.

replies.title: Re: Indigo Children Prediction
replies.content: I confess to having enjoyed Kim Stanley Robinson's novel, "The Days of Rice and Salt," which is an alternate history novel involving a group of 5 or 6 souls who reincarnate together a number of times over a thousand years or so.  I was unaware of the indigo children prediction, and I don't know if it influenced this novel or not.

replies.title: Re: Indigo Children Prediction
replies.content: It was a nice gimmick for a novel, and one I've considered borrowing in different contexts.

replies.title: Re: Indigo Children Prediction
replies.content: That sounds intriguing; I hope you do.

replies.title: Re: Indigo Children Prediction
replies.content: Stay tuned.  ;-)

replies.title: Re: Indigo Children Prediction
replies.content: I agree with our host. I grew up getting more or less regularly doused in that special sauce, even before my household became aware of that particular shade of blue labelling, and I can remember very early memories of tearfully begging my parents to just let me be average. The Indigo thing doesn't seem to me to be much beside that particular form of parenting painted Woo all over.

replies.title: Re: Indigo Children Prediction
replies.content: Yeah, I was tarred and feathered with the label "gifted" from an early age. How that worked out is that no matter how well I did, it was never good enough -- "living up to my potential" was a club constantly used to beat me -- and anything I achieved immediately became the minimum that was acceptable thereafter. I responded by becoming a complete slacker in my teens -- since I wasn't going to get any positive response for anything I did, why try? -- and had to break myself of that habit in my 20s.

replies.title: Re: Indigo Children Prediction
replies.content: Oh man, this is me to a T. I've finally started breaking this habit in my late 20s; do you have any advice for how to accelerate the process of breaking this habit? Thus far, what seems to be working for me is pursuing spiritual practices in complete secrecy: mastering the Golden Dawn is requiring a lot of work, and no one is judging me for it, so I can pursue it without some of my psychological issues.

replies.title: Re: Indigo Children Prediction
replies.content: It was Golden Dawn magic that did the trick for me -- that, and my marriage. (I owe Sara far more than I can ever say; her confidence in me was an immense source of healing and strength in that struggle, among many others.) I wish I had some more general recommendations to offer, but I really don't.

replies.title: Re: Indigo Children Prediction
replies.content:
Not JMG, but in case it helps, a few thoughts on what has seemed to help me some, though I'm not where I'd like to be.

I mitigated much of my teenage and 20s leanings towards slacking by having a pretty good relationship with my parents (so I actually cared that they wanted me to do well in school), and then by finding extrinsic-motivational systems aligned with stuff I actually cared about (I studied subjects I genuinely liked in college, and then I joined the Army for my own reasons). The plus side was that outwardly, I got stuff done and did well, the downside was that eventually I ran out of extrinsic-motivation frameworks I cared about enough.

So, occult work has been helping me learn discipline not because I'll get in trouble or get rewarded for being disciplined, but because I value the results myself. A few other thoughts that have helped/are helping along the way:

1) "The Divinity of Interest": I think this particular phrase came from Jordan Peterson, but basically, if you learn to pay attention to what you actually care about, what grabs your attention, what you do for its own sake, you can start to work with that. Maybe right now that's video games and booze and porn (or whatever else), but maybe scratch at what about those grabs you (besides the obvious, of course). Take video games. Maybe it's the achievement. Maybe it's strategy. Maybe it's competition, or challenge, or exploration. Whatever it is, see if you can find ways to get that that are closer to "productive" for values of productive you actually care about.

2) Small Wins: it can help to identify incremental steps that are more achievable, shoot for those, and celebrate when you get them. Start chaining them together, and you're getting somewhere. Take daily meditation. Maybe you'd like to get to doing it for 20-30 minutes every day, but if you're starting from zero, meditating for five minutes once a week is still doing better. The trick is not to get too comfortable with the incremental steps, but not beat yourself up for staying with them for as long as you need to. Easier said than done, I know.

3) Find a Community that Supports You: Different folks respond differently to peer expectations - for some, its' the make-or-break between success and failure, for others it's a non-factor. But for most folks, it's at least a little helpful for motivation, and if nothing else, it can be practically helpful. That's one thing I've found so helpful about these Magic Mondays - reading about what other folks are doing, asking questions, sharing milestones, and the like have certainly helped me stay on track, so maybe it could help you as well.

Whatever you end up doing, good luck, and hope some of this helps!
Jeff

The swarms -- that's occult theory about how souls descend into manifestation. According to the theories I work with, we -- that is, the souls who inhabit human bodies -- are part of the fourth of seven swarms of souls who will enter into manifestation in this solar system over the course of its history. The first entered manifestation when the solar system was just a dust cloud, and had the task of shaping it into planets and kindling the sun; when they finished their work, they became the Lords of Flame, also known as the angels.

The second swarm descended after that, made the worlds suitable for whatever kind of life would flourish on each of them, and kindled the spark of life; when they were finished, they became the Lords of Form, also known as the devas. The third swarm, who followed, picked up the spark of life and, on this planet -- we don't know enough about the others -- got life to the point of multicellular life forms that souls could fully inhabit; when they were finished, they became the Lords of Mind, also known as the intelligences.

We took it from there. When we're finished, every living thing on earth will be at least as intelligent as we are now, and we will become the Lords of Freedom -- what other name we might be known as hasn't been mentioned in the sources. Then the three future swarms will take it from there, with results we can't even begin to imagine. That's the story of evolution as told in one branch of occult philosophy.

comments.title: A different breed of child
comments.content: The question about Indigo Children is a little timely for me.  I've recently come across the research of Dr. Diane Powell, who has been looking at cases of telepathy in kids, specifically non-verbal autistic children.  When these children are given the ability to communicate via keyboards or other spelling methods, they reveal the fact that they can hear the thoughts of those around them.  And when their parents or care-givers feel that someone is listening to them that is going to believe them when they talk about their experiences, it starts to come clear that it's not just telepathy that these kids are capable of performing.  One teacher or teachers aid, when working with one of the kids, saw a full-blown light-body above the boy and heard in her head "Can you put that back in me where it belongs?" from the student.  I'm honestly just utterly stunned, because the non-verbal autistics and near-non-verbal autistics have so far displayed the entire spectrum of psi-abilities I've ever heard of. They have also stated that the more people know what they can do, the more their capabilities increase.  That, right there, is... terrifying.  Almost as if reality is a consensus. There is an entire podcast right now following the work of Dr. Powell called the Telepathy Tapes that anyone can check out if they have the time or inclination.  And I can only imagine that if you have any NVA individuals in your life, there are people you can reach out to for community.

comments.title: How far can occultism go in our world?
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I was thinking about a fantasy writing project the other day and got to the question: in our own real world, what is possible to do through magical means? How far can our imagination reach without straying out of touch with reality, and what kind of concrete, physical effects can be achieved?

Not sure if this is the same question through different angles or if it's several different questions, sorry!

And thank you for your precious time!

replies.title: Re: How far can occultism go in our world?
replies.content:
I recommend close meditation on Dion Fortune's definition of magic: "Magic is the art and science of causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will." (Notice that this doesn't specify whose consciousness is changing or whose will the changes are in accord with.) Thus magic influences consciousness, not matter; it can influence matter through its effects on conscious beings of various kinds, not all of whom have material bodies, and so the concrete, physical effects that can be achieved are those that are affected by consciousness.

Does that seem unduly limited? States of consciousness can sicken and kill -- look up the phenomenon of voodoo death sometime -- and they can also heal. They can create prosperity and success, and they can create poverty and failure. They can evoke love, hatred, and indifference, draw people together or drive them apart. They played a massive role in electing Donald Trump to the US presidency. All of this and much more is within the reach of magic.

replies.title: Re: How far can occultism go in our world?
replies.content: I also very much like a remark made on this, and your reply, a week or two ago.The implication was/is, that, since we construct the world in which we live in our consciousness based on interpretations of the data we get through our senses and nervous system, actually, changing our consciousness (or anyone's consciousness) isn't limited at all. It can change what's "out there" very much as it changes what's "purely subjective" (whatever those two things mean...)

replies.title: Re: How far can occultism go in our world?
replies.content: That's also quite true -- but I try to dump only one challenging concept on people at a time... ;-)

replies.title: Re: How far can occultism go in our world?
replies.content:
(Original poster here)
Well, this appears to be my stumbling point.
I used to study physics many years ago, and while I learned more about how the world works after leaving college than while I was there (and this was back when college education still meant something), I still seem to have some thought patterns I can't break out of.

What counts as "physical"?
How much of that is generated/influenced by consciousness?
Are the boundaries even knowable?
In fact, is anything at all knowable?
If I let go of that, I feel I'm letting go of reality itself.
In other words, I risk loosing touch with the "real world". In my case, the physical reality as described by physics and inferred by my sensory organs is what grounds me and connects me to Life. That's why I plant trees instead of praying for them, and care for my land and all living things here as my family.
So I guess my deeper question would be:
How far can a human go into the world of magic without falling out the other side? That is, without unwittingly neglecting the physical side of Life and all its manifestations?
I think I'm more or less ready for another step or two, even if I can't understand them fully just yet :)

Thanks again!

replies.title: Re: How far can occultism go in our world?
replies.content:
You're creating a false dichotomy where none needs to exist. You don't have to choose between planting trees and praying for them; you can do both, and I can tell you as a matter of fact that a great many people do this all the time without ill effects. ;-) More broadly, most of the serious occultists I know also hold down regular jobs, have relationships, and function at least as well in the ordinary world as the next person. If anything, their magic makes them more functional in the world, not less.

A crucial point to keep in mind here is that you're actually doing magic all the time. You just aren't conscious of it, and so your magic generally ends up tangled and self-defeating. Since you're doing it anyway, you might as well make it conscious and get good at it.

replies.title: Re: Election 24 occult influence?
replies.content:
There were at least three sets of occult influences at work in this latest election. On the one hand, a lot of the people who got into magic to support Trump's 2016 candidacy, and were swept up in the manifestation of Kek in its final triumphant days, continued to learn about magic, and I have some reason to think a good many of them pursued positive magic thereafter, especially after 2020 -- that is to say, invoking positive energies to strengthen Trump and the United States, along the lines that Dion Fortune advised. That had a significant effect.

comments.title: Buddhist meditation and discursive meditation
comments.content:
I previously noted a few years ago that Tibetan Buddhism has a very similar approach to discursive meditation called "analytical meditation", where the practitioner meditates on a verse, a text in an analytical way.

Through my studies I have come to realise that the broader Buddhist meditation framework actually covers this under the rubrics of shamatha and vipashyana meditation. I use the Sanskrit instead of Pali as I think the Pali terms have become a bit loaded and the Sanskrit is more inclusive of the Mahayana schools anyway.

The main confusion that people have is that shamatha often takes the fore, or some modern approaches to vipashyana, such as Goenka's method, have come to dominate the meaning of the term while being very idiosyncratic.

Shamatha practice brings about a focused and concentrated mind, and the focus produced from entering and exiting very deep states of meditation is very strong. These are the dhyānas. They are not strictly necessary according to Buddhism but they are just very helpful for the deep focus which they bring about.

With the focus produced by these strong states of meditation, one then analyses the component's of one's consciousness in vipashyana.

Discursive meditation as taught by JMG also has a short period of breathing, with the same effect as shamatha, but without the explicit goal of deep meditative states.

Then the discursive meditation itself is analogous to vipashyana, just the themes differ.

Anyway just putting this out there to share.

comments.title: Streams of Thought

I usually meditate while walking works very good especially when I'm tired. There's one thing that's happening, though, which may have it's origins in my long-term habit of going for a walk and think about stuff. It's usually not random wandering of the mind but comes along in form of a coherent monologue or dialogue and stays. If I interrupt it too early, it will leak into my regular meditation. It requires no conscious effort. If I let it run it's course it will run out after a while (which can be short or up to 30 minutes) and then usually a productive meditation of the chosen subject follows.

I know it's encouraged to quiet the mind during meditation, on the other hand rhythmic breathing seems to give my subconscious the opportunity to say a few things it feels to be important. comments.title: Self, Image and Reality
comments.content:
Hi JMG, in your answer to a question last week (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/302895.html?thread=52029743#cmt52029743) you said that "The fixation on a single image is a good way to see to it that the opposite of that image manifests in one's life." Do you have any thoughts as to the causes of this? Is a person fixating on an identity image unlike their own feeding their energy to the egregore of that image rather than cultivating its traits in themselves?

I can think of many examples of this I've seen, like people who buy into multilevel marketing pyramid schemes and get obsessed with "dream building" their vision of themselves as millionaires while their lives collapse around them. There are scores of others. Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Self, Image and Reality
replies.content:
I too am curious about this, from the direction of how it differs from focused will in magic.
Fixation on an image leading to the opposite of the image manifesting seems strange when so much of magic is about unified focus and will on a singular goal. Is the difference in positive vs negative fixation? Or, perhaps in the consciousness of fixation?

replies.title: Re: Self, Image and Reality
replies.content:
The magical will, properly applied, is surgical. It focuses on one specific change at a time, or on one goal at a time, and it's also always manifested in practice on the material plane.

Consider somebody who wants to get rich. If they do it the New Age way, they fixate on imagery of themselves being rich, getting lots of money out of thin air, and then sit and wait for it to happen. Some New Age teachers actually advise their students to start spending money as though they're already rich, on the assumption that the universe will follow suit; even among those that don't, I've yet to encounter a New Age teacher who says, "Set a budget and get to work raising the money."

If they do it the magical way, they set a specific goal for themselves -- earning one million dollars above expenses, let's say -- and do some form of working for that goal. They also act on it on the material plane, by establishing a savings plan, cutting their expenses, and seeking out opportunities to make money. Notice how all these latter steps start from the realization "I'm not rich yet" -- see how that makes the balance work out? You have to accept where you are before you can go where you want to be.

replies.title: Re: Self, Image and Reality
replies.content:
That makes sense. So, the difference is in perspective and execution.
Fixation on an image, without clear perception of the greater patterns surrounding the image, and without action taken to work within that pattern, is likely to drive one away from the goal rather than toward it.
This feels like a corollary of "asking spirits for stuff."
Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Self, Image and Reality
replies.content: The universe is governed by the law of equilibrium, the principle that Taoists symbolize with yin and yang. If you push one way all the time, the universe will swing around and shove you the other way. You have to embrace both poles of each polarity. That's why, for example, you'll have a much easier time getting rich if you live as though you're poorer than you are, and why the ability to be comfortable in solitude is the best preparation for a relationship. William Walker Atkinson's classic The Kybalion is a good source here; he explains how to use the laws of polarity and rhythm to get what you want in life without getting clobbered by the return to equilibrium.

comments.title: Vultures bearing messages
comments.content:
Dear JMG and commentariat,

The week before the election I went for a stroll in a pasture on the state university grounds where I work, and a vulture approached me. Just walked right up and looked at me meaningfully (?) for a couple minutes. Giant bird as big as a goose with iridescent brown feathers and a naked pinkish grey head. I was a little freaked out—vultures are big!!!

I spoke to it a bit. It just looked at me. Amazing eye contact (can I reemphasize how uncanny vultures are?) We parted ways.

Later I called the state ornithologist and she said there had been many unusual reports of vultures on campus lately.

This is a major American university in a blue state. Symbolically vultures signify death and transformation. Considering I was not the only one to encounter the bird, it seems as if the phenomenon may be collective…perhaps American academia is in for significant changes.

I’m open to anyone else’s ideas of the significance, too.

—Ms. Krieger

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you!

For anyone interested: Mostly what toads seem to need is shelter in a shady spot that doesn’t receive direct sunlight. It should be bottomless so that they are not prevented from burrowing into the dirt (or, less ideally, it can be something with a bottom partially buried and then infilled with dirt, like a flowerpot on its side half-buried). A few bricks topped with a flat stone or piece of wood will do. A perennial source of water for breeding is ideal, but they can travel some distance to this when the time comes (up to 2 or 3 miles in some cases). A saucer or shallow puddle nearby is also appreciated. Their shelter should have some leaf litter on its floor for bedding material. You can even put a toad light out to attract bugs for them, although I probably won’t, as I don’t like having artificial lights about. They can apparently live for over a decade and will often return over the long-term if they find a nice shelter!

JMG: It occurs to me that I haven’t read much about these sorts of magical properties of animals (as compared with plants or stones, say). Is there a source you would recommend? Or do you know of any other animals (less elusive than the basilisk or tard!) who share this property of absorbing noxious magic with the toad? Is it any animal of Saturn? Levi lists hoopoe, squid, and mole as being associated with Saturn. I’ve also heard that black animals, burrowing animals, and cats belong to Saturn, but I’m not sure how reliable that is. Levi lists cats as being ruled by the Moon, not Saturn.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There's a lot of medieval lore about animals, but nearly all of it presupposes killing the animal and using parts of its body, and for wholly personal reasons that's not lore I'm willing to spread around. I don't think the energy-absorbing power of toads is an effect of their Saturnine nature, for what it's worth. In the traditional lore of natural magic everything has two categories of effect, one that derives from its correspondences and one that is specific to it and it alone. (For example, emerald is a stone of Venus and has the general effect of amplifying all Venus's influences, but it has the specific effect of encouraging lasting love.) As far as I know, the power of toads to soak up nasty magic is specific, not generic.

Hildegard Von Bingen compiled a *lot* of medieval lore on animals in her *Physica*. A lot of it is, of course, pretty inscrutable to us non-medieval folk.

I find the American form of Santa Claus repellent—repulsive even—and I would go so far to say, wicked and spiritually dangerous. He’s a Satanic consumerist Antichrist figure. I think you mostly agree with me, which is refreshing!

I love and revere St. Nicholas (I’m an Orthodox Christian) and have imparted this to my children. I’ve managed to keep Santa at bay overall.

However. My mother-in-law is a Santa Claus devotee who does her utmost to push him on my kids. My husband is ambivalent and rather passive about it, and maybe thinks my Santa-rejection is a little silly, but he is good about respecting my wishes. Because we rarely spend Christmas with his mother, it doesn’t usually come up. Our kids show no interest in Santa at all—my oldest actually described him as “scary” at age 2, before I even fully figured out my own unease about him.

But this year my mother-in-law insisted on buying very expensive, non-refundable tickets for a social media-y Santa event near our home. We tried every which way to say “no” politely—right up to being arguably rude—and she completely tuned us out and bought the tickets anyway. In hindsight I should have started with a rude, hard “no”, but I had no idea she would rebuff our polite declines so completely. I have learned my lesson. At this point there is no way out of it, because my husband kindly told me to get over it and back off (and it is his mother, and it is “just Santa”.)

For the record, our kids wanted to do something else entirely with their grandmother while she was briefly in town, and will be disappointed that the other holiday event is no longer really a possibility because she overrode that with this all-afternoon Santa event—so I don’t feel like I’m being a killjoy by objecting.

Anyway, it seems unavoidable now so I’m wondering what you would do to counter Santa’s influence on a (natural) magical level. Our children are little so nothing heavy. My idea: prayer, banishing (SOP), maybe a ritual bath afterwards. And emphasizing St. Nicholas, Christ, Mary, and the saints in a big way, as we do already. Do you have any other suggestions? Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you're a practicing Christian, your best bet is prayer -- to Christ, the Virgin Mary, St. Nicholas, and also to St. Michael the Archangel, who protects against evil. You can also talk to your kids after the event is over and help them process the experience; a lot of children think that Santa Claus is creepy, so this can be a learning experience for them. They're going to get exposed to the Spirit of Yuletide Greed sooner or later, after all.

This is interesting, because I'd had the impression that racialist Paganism was on the decline, having peaked close to twenty years ago. Still, it may be going through another resurgence in some circles. I recommend, if you haven't read it already, Nicholas Goodrick-Clarke's book Black Sun, which does a fine job of setting out the history of the movement from a well-informed academic standpoint.

My take is that, no, the gods are not fighting against Christianity. Gods are eternal, and the temporal vagaries of human religion are of little if any concern to them. Too many young men these days turn to racialist beliefs because of the Pygmalion effect -- having been told over and over again that they're privileged racists who are personally responsible for everything bad their ancestors ever did, it's hardly surprising that they should end up acting out the role that's been projected so stridently onto them.

Nor, by the way, is Christianity as welcoming as all that. It doesn't exclude ethnicities, granted, but like most prophetic religions it excludes anyone who can't agree with a long list of doctrinal claims and behavioral demands. Pagan religions, interestingly, didn't and don't have that problem; every person in attendance at a Roman ceremony in 200 BC or a Shinto ceremony today may have a different notion concerning just what the numina or kami are, and other theological questions, but that didn't/doesn't prevent them from worshipping together in perfect amity.

replies.content:
As a Heathen myself, I thought I might add a couple of items for perspective, in case they help. It's certainly the case that there are folks who view Germanic polytheism as a racial thing, and/or use it to justify whatever racial thing they might have going otherwise. It's out there, and it's got a long and ugly history, so you're not wrong to notice something going on. Also as JMG said, though, my impression from most of my online interactions is that that's not where most of the energy is, though that may come down to where I spend my time.

A couple of factors might also contribute to an impression of racially-charged Germanic polytheism. The first is the effect of various social and political currents going on in the world, and how those have interacted with the "Neo-Pagan" scene. Those Heathen groups that are more comfortable with those currents often proudly proclaim themselves as "universalist" - the Gods are real, so why shouldn't anyone who feels called to do so worship them? This tends to align with left-leaning social trends and political leanings. Many Heathens, though, aren't entirely onboard with those social trends and don't share those political leanings, and in seeking to differentiate themselves, reject the "universalist" label. If you're not aware of the history or context, you might see someone talking disparagingly of "universalism" and assume they must be racist - possibly, but not necessarily.

Related to this, some Heathens believe that different ethnic groups can and should have their own religious traditions, and ask "why not for northern Europeans, too?" These folks call themselves "folkish," as "folk" is the Germanic word for "people" and can be used in the same way as the Latin-derived word to either mean "a group of persons" or "a group of persons united by ethnic identity." At the more enlightened end, you get something like Iamblichus's take that different peoples have different habits, virtues, faults, and characters, and so they have rituals and Gods suited to those things, even if beyond those Gods is some more universal divinity. At the uglier end of things, yeah, some of these people are racists, but again, from outside, it can be easy to assume that anyone who thinks ethnicity has any role in religion must be doing it for racist reasons.

Anyhow, all of that is to say that you might indeed be seeing some folks use Germanic religion as a vehicle for pushing racist views, but that it's possible there are some other things going on that might give that impression without necessarily being exactly that.

Hope some of this is helpful, or at least interesting,
Jeff

I think you've said that Saturn Returns are generally better for learning what your problems are and less great for actually resolving them. What is your advice on the Return - am I too paranoid RE the consequences of not succeeding during it?

2. I got two geomancy charts that essentially outline two paths I can take in life. Let's call the first chart "A":

https://georatio.com/?c=a76f&t=1&d=7&s=2&q=

And the second chart "B":

https://georatio.com/?c=2947&t=1&d=7&s=2&q=

I believe these are answered in the form of whole house charts, not as questions pertaining to specific houses, but as that's too much information to analyze in one post, I would like your opinion which path seems better based on the Witnesses and Judge and the figure in the 4th house. To my mind, both charts are only weakly positive, though Tristitia and Puer are both generally negative figures.

3. Much like any human in incarnation, I have many desires. Some of them I fully realize are counter-useful and yet they keep popping up. Meditation helps me become more aware of them, but they can get the better of me when I'm highly anxious or energized. And I sometimes act very impulsively first thing in the morning, to my regret, based on anxieties and unrealistic desires. Do you have general recommendations for dealing with episodes of high anxiety and energy? FYI, I use red cloth amulets, take cold showers, do a daily Ritual of the Pentagram, and try to exercise and get sunlight daily. I'm thinking forcing a cold shower and hard bike ride as soon as I wake up would be especially beneficial if I have problems with my impulses first thing in the morning, but any other advice helps.

4. One more question: regarding conversing with the beings you meet in dreams and nightmares, could you repeat the process for how you go about that? I'm becoming better at interpreting my dreams, but I like the idea of attempting to converse with the beings I meet so I can better understand myself and what I should do in the future. Because I know my dreams have given me useful information that I didn't act on before.

replies.content:
1) Anything that seems like an imperative need during a Saturn return is an opportunity to find out what it feels like to walk face first into a brick wall. Assume that everything you try during this time is likely to fail, and that you'll have plenty of opportunities to be more successful once Saturn moves on, and you'll have better results.

2) A is better than B, but not by much. You might consider other options -- or simply wait until your Saturn return is over.

3) I find journaling useful for this, though of course your mileage may vary.

4) If you want to do this kind of work, I very strongly recommend The Jungian-Senoi Dreamwork Manual by Strephon Kaplan Williams. It gives a comprehensive toolkit of dream methods including that one, and covers it in much more detail than I can fit into a brief comment here. It's readily available on the used book market.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First, I do Magic Monday every week, and it runs for 24 hours, from the midnight when Sunday ends all the way around until the midnight when Tuesday begins. So you don't have to worry -- it'll be here next week.

Second, relax. The last thing you should do is rush into a relationship with any deity.

Third, if you're not a Wiccan, then Wiccan rules don't apply to you. It's as simple as that.

Okay, now that we've covered that, let's go on. If you don't have a lot of background with the old gods and goddesses, I recommend that you go to the library, get some introductory books of myths -- books written for older kids and young adults are often very good -- and read them, to get a sense of what myths and what deities attract you. You can and should read myths from many different cultures. Greek and Norse myths are the easiest to come by, but with a little looking you can find all kinds of myths from around the world, and that'll give you a sense of the deeper realities underlying the old stories.

Once you've spent some time doing that, you'll be in a much better position to start thinking about who you might want to invoke. So there's your homework for this week!
